evidence: Guard clauses throughout are accompanied by explanatory comments (e.g. extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition position<1 note, extractCollocateLemma collocatePos>0 check) making intent clear

In executeBcqlQuery / collectHits, collocatePos == -1 is handled via explicit > 0 guard in extractCollocateLemma with fallback to extractLastLemma; in executeSurfacePattern, the same -1 return passes directly to extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition and extractPlainTextTokenAt, both of which return null for position < 1, silently producing empty results with no logging

The comment in BlackLabSnippetParser.extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition claims BcqlPatternUtils.findLabelTokenIndex() 'returns >= 1', but CqlUtils.findLabelTokenIndex actually returns -1 on failure, creating a false contract that could mislead future callers

VisualizationHandlers.RenderMode.isValid hardcodes the literal string 'SIGNED' instead of using enum values/valueOf; a new enum constant would silently bypass validation without any compile-time signal
Boolean/branching logic in SemanticFieldExplorer (filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates), MultiSeedExplorer, and CollocateProfileComparator is clear and each phase is well-named
Main.parseIntParam / parseDoubleParam use System.exit(1) followed by throw new AssertionError('unreachable') — standard Java idiom, comment present, not a clarity defect
No dead code paths after unconditional returns, no always-true/false conditions, no identical if/else branches were found across the reviewed files

strengths: Guard clauses are consistently accompanied by explanatory comments that justify their presence (e.g. BlackLabSnippetParser position<1 note, CollocateQueryHelper OVER_FETCH_FACTOR comment), so defensive code never reads as noise

Control-flow phasing in SemanticFieldExplorer (fetchSeedCollocates, buildNounToCollocatesMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) names each algorithmic phase explicitly, making the flow self-documenting

The extractCollocateLemma method in CollocateQueryHelper correctly handles the -1 sentinel from findLabelTokenIndex with an explicit > 0 guard and a named fallback, showing awareness of the failure mode
No dead code paths after unconditional returns, no always-true/always-false conditions, and no identical if/else branches were found across the 22 reviewed files
logic_clarity.issue_character: The issues are isolated, edge-case inconsistencies rather than systemic: one callsite (executeSurfacePattern) silently swallows a -1 sentinel that a sibling callsite (executeBcqlQuery) handles explicitly, and a single-value enum's isValid method duplicates a hardcoded string instead of deriving from the enum — both confined to one file each.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase is mostly clean from a logic-clarity perspective: guards are explained, algorithm phases are well-named, and no broad-pattern defects were found. What pulls the score below 80 is one genuine inconsistency — executeSurfacePattern silently produces empty results when findLabelTokenIndex returns -1, while executeBcqlQuery correctly handles the same sentinel — plus a false comment in BlackLabSnippetParser that misrepresents the sentinel value's contract. These are narrow but real: a developer relying on the comment or mirroring the executeSurfacePattern pattern in a new callsite could introduce a hard-to-spot silent failure. A solid 78 reflects code that does what it claims across the vast majority of paths, with a small cluster of low-severity but real logic-clarity gaps.

issues.summary: executeSurfacePattern silently drops all groups when findLabelTokenIndex returns -1 (label 2: absent), while the sibling executeBcqlQuery path handles the same sentinel with an explicit fallback
evidence: CqlUtils.findLabelTokenIndex returns -1 when the label is not found (line 'return -1;' in both early-exit and fall-through paths)
In CollocateQueryHelper.collectHits, collocatePos is passed to extractCollocateLemma which guards with 'if (collocatePos > 0)' and falls back to extractLastLemma — -1 is handled gracefully

In BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern, collocatePos is passed directly into the lambda: extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition(identity, collocatePos) then extractPlainTextTokenAt(identity, collocatePos); both methods guard 'position < 1' and return null, causing every group to be skipped with no warning log
The two callsites exhibit different behavior for the identical -1 sentinel, making the logic of executeSurfacePattern inconsistent with the established pattern
issues.suggestion: Add an explicit check in executeSurfacePattern after CqlUtils.findLabelTokenIndex: if collocatePos < 1, either log a debug/warn message and return early with an empty list, or mirror extractCollocateLemma's fallback (collocatePos > 0 guard + extractLastLemma fallback) in the lambda. Aligning both callsites removes the silent failure mode and makes the -1 case visible.

issues.summary: BlackLabSnippetParser comment claims findLabelTokenIndex 'returns >= 1', but the method returns -1 on failure, creating a false contract that misleads future callers
evidence: BlackLabSnippetParser.extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition comment: 'all current callers pass values from BcqlPatternUtils.findLabelTokenIndex() which returns >= 1, or explicitly check > 0 before calling'
CqlUtils.findLabelTokenIndex (the successor to BcqlPatternUtils) returns -1 in two places: when pattern is null, and when the label is not found
The comment's first clause 'returns >= 1' is false for the not-found case; the class name 'BcqlPatternUtils' is stale (the method now lives in CqlUtils)
A developer reading the comment could incorrectly conclude that no > 0 guard is needed when calling extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition in a new callsite
issues.suggestion: Update the comment to accurately reflect the actual sentinel: 'CqlUtils.findLabelTokenIndex returns -1 when the label is absent; callers must check position > 0 before calling this method.' Also update the stale class reference from BcqlPatternUtils to CqlUtils.

issues.summary: VisualizationHandlers.RenderMode.isValid hardcodes the literal string 'SIGNED' instead of deriving from enum values, so adding a new enum constant would silently bypass validation
evidence: enum RenderMode { SIGNED; static boolean isValid(String value) { return "SIGNED".equalsIgnoreCase(value); } }
The isValid method implies 'validates against the defined enum modes' but actually only validates against a single hardcoded string literal
If a second mode (e.g. UNSIGNED) is added to the enum, isValid will continue to reject it unless manually updated — the enum definition and the validation are decoupled with no compile-time linkage
issues.suggestion: Replace the hardcoded string check with an enum-based lookup: try { RenderMode.valueOf(value.toUpperCase()); return true; } catch (IllegalArgumentException e) { return false; } — or use Arrays.stream(RenderMode.values()).anyMatch(m -> m.name().equalsIgnoreCase(value)). Either approach keeps isValid automatically in sync with the enum's defined constants.

root_causes: The -1 sentinel contract for findLabelTokenIndex was not consistently propagated when the utility moved from BcqlPatternUtils to CqlUtils, leaving one callsite unguarded
The RenderMode enum was written with a single value and the validation method hardcoded it; no convention exists to keep enum-based validation in sync with enum constants
likely_symptoms: surface_pattern_neg1_silent_drop may manifest as unexpectedly empty word-sketch results when grammar patterns lack a 2: label — rare but silent
